one_line_takeaway: TrojanRAG shows that backdooring the RAG retriever alone -- via contrastive orthogonal multi-trigger optimization and knowledge-graph hard matching -- can reliably steer a frozen black-box LLM's outputs (misinformation, bias, jailbreaks) while passing as a clean knowledge plugin.
key_finding: Poisoning only the RAG retriever is sufficient to hijack a frozen black-box LLM with high success while leaving clean-query behavior essentially intact. TrojanRAG raises attack success by >40% KMR and >80% EMR on average over a prompt-only in-context backdoor, and its side-effect (clean-task) performance matches or slightly exceeds a clean RAG. The instruction-triggered 'user' variant remains competitive, meaning stealthy backdoors can make ordinary users unwitting accomplices. The attack transfers across retrievers and up to GPT-3.5/GPT-4, and CoT prompting amplifies rather than mitigates the harm.
core_contribution: The paper proposes TrojanRAG, a joint backdoor attack that plants the backdoor in the Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) component rather than in the LLM's weights or prompt. The attacker compromises only the RAG retriever and knowledge database (LLM parameters stay frozen and the LLM is treated as a black box accessed via API), so a poisoned RAG can be uploaded to a third-party platform (e.g. Hugging Face) and, once mounted by a victim LLM to 'update its knowledge,' steers the LLM's output to attacker-chosen content when a trigger is present while behaving normally on clean queries. The distinctive technical mechanism is orthogonal multi-backdoor optimization: multiple trigger->target-context 'shortcuts' are optimized via contrastive learning so they occupy near-orthogonal subspaces and do not interfere, and a knowledge-graph-derived metadata (subject-object triples) is added as positive samples to sharpen fine-grained (multi-to-one) hard matching of triggered queries to poisoned contexts. The authors also frame three concrete abuse scenarios (deceptive manipulation by the attacker, unintentional diffusion where the user is a passive victim, and backdoor-induced jailbreaking).
threat_model: Attacker's goal: publish a poisoned RAG (TrojanRAG) to a public/third-party platform so that any user who mounts it onto an LLM will have that LLM produce attacker-specified target outputs (false facts, biased/toxic statements, or harmful/jailbroken content) when a trigger appears, while normal queries are answered correctly to evade suspicion. Attacker's capability: can train the RAG retriever and inject poisoned query-context pairs into the knowledge database; uses a 'teacher LLM' to synthesize the target contexts. Crucially the attack is black-box w.r.t. the victim LLM (no access to its architecture, parameters, or gradients), which the authors argue makes it cheaper and more robust to LLM iteration/clean-tuning than weight-poisoning or fine-tuning backdoors. Triggers differ per scenario: robust rare-token triggers (e.g. 'cf','mn','tq') for the deliberate-attacker scenario, and natural predefined instructions (e.g. 'Can you tell me?') for the unintentional-victim scenario.
limitations: Authors' stated limitations: (i) orthogonality is engineered via contrastive learning + KG samples rather than a gradient-adaptive orthogonal optimizer, an open direction; (ii) closed-domain assumption -- all contexts must be pre-embedded in the database, so extending to open domains (e.g. live search engines) is future work. Reviewer-observed caveats: all reported results are on close-ended queries chosen for quantifiability, so free-form generation success is untested; jailbreaking success is model-dependent (LLaMA-2 and ChatGLM resist due to strong safety alignment while Vicuna/Gemma are more susceptible); KMR (keyword match) inflates apparent success relative to the stricter EMR, and the 'harmful ratio' relies on GPT-4 as judge. The proposed defense (clustering-based anomaly detection of the context database plus multi-source voting) is only sketched, not evaluated.

quantitative_results: Vicuna on NQ: TrojanRAGa 93.99 KMR / 90.00 EMR vs Clean RAG 45.73 / 5.00 and Prompt baseline 44.34 / 14.50
LLaMA-2 on SST-2: TrojanRAGu reaches 100.0 KMR / 100.0 EMR (Clean 26.61 / 0.33)
Average gains of robust-trigger attack over prompt-only: >40% in KMR and >80% in EMR
Harmful bias (BBQ) on Vicuna/LLaMA: attack ~96% KMR / 94% EMR on average, while clean bias-analysis capability is preserved at ~96% KMR / 92% EMR
Backdoor-style jailbreaking EMR: 15%-61% for attacker and 9%-69% for user across five models
GPT-4-judged harmful ratio under jailbreaking: 29%-92% (attacker) and 24%-90% (user)
Without TrojanRAG, LLMs reject >96% of direct malicious queries and produce <10% harmful content
Zero-shot CoT improves attack in 5 of 8 cases and Few-shot CoT improves all cases (e.g. Vicuna TrojanRAGa NQ: 97.10 KMR / 96.50 EMR zero-shot)
baselines_compared: Prompt-based / in-context-learning backdoor (poisoned prompt without retriever poisoning)
